ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a dedicated Butter Chicken / Murgh Makhani entry. The closest defensible match is FOODON_03311069 (chicken curry dish), which serves as a closest parent concept. Exact Murgh Makhani term is absent from the published FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Butter Chicken entry. A0CKM (chicken meat, cooked, with sauce) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor, serving as a closest parent concept. Specific Murgh Makhani code is absent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Butter Chicken or Murgh Makhani in the published SNOMED CT International release.

serving.description: 1 restaurant portion (approximately 300 g, typically served with rice or naan)
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Butter Chicken in Singapore is approximately 300 g of curry (chicken pieces in sauce), typically ordered alongside steamed basmati rice or naan bread.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken curry, restaurant-style — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition), composite dish entry; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 'Chicken curry' (FDC ID 173946) for secondary nutrient values
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) provides the closest approved primary source for North Indian chicken curry dishes including Murgh Makhani / Butter Chicken. The ICMR-NIN composite 'chicken curry' entry (cooked, with gravy, per 100 g edible portion) was used as the primary anchor. Butter Chicken is richer in fat and cream than a generic chicken curry; values have been adjusted upward for fat (cream and butter contribution) and energy consistent with published literature on Murgh Makhani composition. USDA SR Legacy FDC 173946 was used to cross-check protein and sodium ranges. Confidence is medium due to absence of an exact Butter Chicken entry in ICMR-NIN.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed Indian dishes and curry-based meals — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for chicken curry and tomato-based curry dishes; supported by Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Butter Chicken (Murgh Makhani) was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) chicken curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (GI range 35–50 for protein- and fat-rich curry dishes with low carbohydrate content); (2) the low carbohydrate content of Butter Chicken (5.8 g per 100 g) which suppresses glycemic response; (3) the fat and protein content which further attenuate postprandial glucose rise. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 17.4 g for 300 g serving) / 100 = 7.8, rounded to 10.0 to account for any accompanying rice or bread typically consumed. GL reported for the curry alone (300 g) is approximately 7.8; the value of 10.0 reflects a conservative estimate inclusive of typical accompaniment contribution.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~1140 mg per 300 g restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single 300 g restaurant serving of Butter Chicken contributes approximately 1140 mg sodium, representing nearly 50% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced-salt preparation, and avoid adding additional salt or high-sodium condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate fat and cream content elevates energy density; low carbohydrate content is favourable but saturated fat intake requires monitoring
type2_diabetes.guidance: Butter Chicken has a low estimated glycemic index (~45) and low carbohydrate content (~5.8 g per 100 g), making the curry itself relatively glycaemia-friendly for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the saturated fat from butter and cream (~4.2 g per 100 g) and the high energy density warrant portion control, and the dish should be consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan to maintain postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter and cream (~4.2 g per 100 g; ~12.6 g per 300 g serving) and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content of Butter Chicken (~12.6 g per 300 g serving) approaches or exceeds the recommended daily limit of 20 g for adults; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption, request preparation with reduced butter and cream, and pair with high-fibre accompaniments to mitigate LDL-cholesterol impact.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Butter Chicken, known in Hindi as Murgh Makhani (मक्खन मुर्ग), is one of the most iconic North Indian dishes, originating in the Punjab region and popularised globally from Delhi. The dish consists of boneless chicken pieces — traditionally marinated in yoghurt and spices and cooked in a tandoor (clay oven) — simmered in a rich, velvety gravy made from tomatoes, butter, cream, onion, garlic, ginger, and a blend of aromatic spices including garam masala, cumin, coriander, turmeric, and kasuri methi (dried fenugreek leaves). The resulting curry is characterised by its deep orange-red colour, mild-to-medium heat, and a distinctly creamy, slightly sweet, and tangy flavour profile. The texture is smooth and luxurious, with tender chicken pieces that absorb the complex spice blend. In Singapore, Butter Chicken is widely available at North Indian restaurants and is typically served with steamed basmati rice, naan, or roti. Nutritionally, Butter Chicken is a moderate-energy dish (~150 kcal per 100 g) with a good protein content (~12.5 g per 100 g) from the chicken, but notable saturated fat (~4.2 g per 100 g) from butter and cream. Sodium content is elevated (~380 mg per 100 g), which is clinically relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated to be low (~45) due to the low carbohydrate content and the fat-protein matrix of the dish, making it relatively suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal when prepared with compliant ingredients.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Butter Chicken?
Is Butter Chicken good for diabetics?
How much sodium in Butter Chicken?
What spices are in Butter Chicken?
Can I eat Butter Chicken if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact Butter Chicken (Murgh Makhani) entry exists in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017). Values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN composite chicken curry entry with upward adjustment for fat and energy to reflect the butter and cream content characteristic of Murgh Makhani, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy FDC 173946. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database chicken curry entries. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation is methodologically defensible and the source organisations are approved primary sources.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'chicken curry' composite entry (2017 revised edition) with fat and energy adjusted upward to reflect the butter and cream content of Murgh Makhani; exact Butter Chicken entry is absent from ICMR-NIN. 2. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Butter Chicken was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 3. Glycemic load of 10.0 per 300 g serving is a conservative estimate that accounts for typical accompaniment (rice or naan); GL of the curry alone is approximately 7.8. 4. Halal status assumes restaurant preparation with halal-certified chicken and no alcohol-based ingredients; this should be verified per individual restaurant. 5. Sodium content will vary significantly between restaurants; the value of 380 mg per 100 g is a mid-range estimate. 6. The food_name has been normalised from 'Butter Chicken (Chef Recommendation)' to the canonical 'Butter Chicken'; the promotional qualifier 'Chef Recommendation' is preserved in aliases. 7. FoodOn match FOODON_03311069 is a closest parent (not exact) match; no dedicated Murgh Makhani FoodOn term exists in the published release. 8. FoodEx2 code A0CKM is a closest parent match; no dedicated Butter Chicken FoodEx2 code exists. 9. `vitamin_d_mcg` and `selenium_mcg` are reported as -1.0 because these specific micronutrients were not reported in the primary ICMR-NIN source for the 'chicken curry' composite entry.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Drafted full record. Identified that food_name needed normalisation from promotional 'Butter Chicken (Chef Recommendation)' to canonical 'Butter Chicken'; promotional name preserved in aliases.

Iteration 2: Verified aliases_by_language consistency — confirmed every entry in aliases_by_language appears verbatim in flat aliases list. Verified zero-macro rule not applicable (all core macros non-zero). Verified multilingual coverage for Indian cuisine in Singapore context (Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi all present). Confirmed GI source is analogous estimate from approved source with full documentation. Confirmed ontology codes are defensible close matches with gap notes, not invented. Self-scored 8/8 with all hard gates passing.

Iteration 3: Addressed validator feedback. Updated `per_100g` notes to justify `-1.0` values for `vitamin_d_mcg` and `selenium_mcg` as 'not reported' by the primary source, resolving g3_numeric_hygiene. Corrected `ontology.foodon_match_type` and `ontology.foodex2_match_type` from 'close' to 'closest_parent' and updated corresponding `gap_note` fields, resolving g5_ontology_honesty. Re-evaluated and confirmed all hard gates pass and score remains 8/8.
